SEARCH
Module DSA
Package com.dsa.pattern

Class UnionFindDisjointSet

java.lang.Object
com.dsa.pattern.UnionFindDisjointSet
public class UnionFindDisjointSet extends Object
Demonstrates the Union-Find / Disjoint Set Pattern.

Union-Find (also called Disjoint Set Union, DSU) is a data structure that keeps track of a set of elements partitioned into disjoint subsets. It supports two main operations efficiently:

  • Find: Determine which subset a particular element belongs to
  • Union: Merge two subsets into a single subset

Key Idea

  • Use an array `parent[]` where `parent[i]` is the parent of element i
  • Optionally, use `rank[]` or `size[]` to optimize union operations
  • Path compression in `find` ensures nearly O(1) amortized operations

When to Use

  • Connected components in undirected graphs
  • Kruskal’s algorithm for Minimum Spanning Tree
  • Checking if adding an edge creates a cycle
  • Dynamic connectivity problems

Example Implementation


 package com.dsa.pattern;

 public class UnionFindDisjointSetExample {

     static class UnionFind {
         int[] parent, rank;

         UnionFind(int n) {
             parent = new int[n];
             rank = new int[n];
             for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) parent[i] = i;
         }

         int find(int x) {
             if (parent[x] != x) parent[x] = find(parent[x]); // path compression
             return parent[x];
         }

         void union(int x, int y) {
             int rootX = find(x);
             int rootY = find(y);
             if (rootX == rootY) return;

             if (rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]) {
                 parent[rootX] = rootY;
             } else if (rank[rootX] > rank[rootY]) {
                 parent[rootY] = rootX;
             } else {
                 parent[rootY] = rootX;
                 rank[rootX]++;
             }
         }
     }

     public static void main(String[] args) {
         UnionFind uf = new UnionFind(5);

         uf.union(0, 1);
         uf.union(1, 2);
         uf.union(3, 4);

         System.out.println("Find(0): " + uf.find(0)); // same as Find(2)
         System.out.println("Find(2): " + uf.find(2));
         System.out.println("Find(3): " + uf.find(3));

         System.out.println("Are 0 and 2 connected? " + (uf.find(0) == uf.find(2)));
         System.out.println("Are 0 and 4 connected? " + (uf.find(0) == uf.find(4)));
     }
 }

Complexity

  • Time Complexity: Nearly O(1) per operation (amortized)
  • Space Complexity: O(n) for parent and rank arrays

Applications

  • Connected components in undirected graphs
  • Kruskal’s Minimum Spanning Tree algorithm
  • Cycle detection in graphs
  • Dynamic connectivity queries
